story:
Brooke had turned daydreaming into a ritual, sitting with her messy blonde curls and faded jeans, gazing at the wreckage left by her sister’s giant, lovable hound: dog biscuit crumbs ground into the pile, drifting clots of fur, suspicious little stains, even the telltale streaks of a naughty backside on a beige carpet. Her old vacuum wheezed, so she did what her girlfriends swore by: bought the top-of-the-line robot cleaner, programmed it, and believed the ads. This sleek, quiet frisbee promised to glide under beds and sofas, vacuum, mop tile, even dust skirting boards. She set it free. It hummed, it polished, it defluffed. The digital display flashed a jaunty thumbs-up. For a moment, Brooke felt the first-world future arrive.

While it worked, she drifted online to the very hot store manager, Edward—kissable lips, probably too good to be true, but still: a girl could dream. If she saved up, she might even add the window-and-shower model and banish chores for good. Between fantasies and emojis, she told herself she believed in a glittering tomorrow, even if she carried soft longings for something warmer than a clean floor.

Sunday nights brought her sister, the kids, and the hound back over. Mess followed. No problem—she could rise ten minutes early, unleash the miracle machine, and go teach. Some school days are pearls; that one was. She came home happy—then stepped into three inches of water. The house sloshed. From the family room came a frantic banging. The robot was ramming the patio glass where wild birds gathered for their feed, its display blinking little bird wings and: “Sigh freedom.” Had the thing caught feelings? More likely, a sensor glitch and a mop cycle gone rogue, but Brooke, who was gentle with everything, spoke softly anyway: “You’re a very naughty robot, but you’re having big feelings. Let it out.”

When she reached for it, the robot skittered away and hid under the couch, whirring like a defiant pet and spitting out fluff, dog hair, crumbs, and a couple of unfortunate spiders. “Great, robot, great.” She coaxed it out with a broom, soaked her work slacks, and accepted that the repair shop would have to wait. She texted a co-teacher to cover her morning class, cranked the heat, and sweated through the night as the carpet refused to dry. 

Now it was tomorrow, as Brooke walked into the robot store the minute it opened. The robot rolled onto the counter with a sad-face emoji and “Need repair.” Edward, reassuring and capable, took the machine under his arm and offered to clock out early, bring over a new “super sopper” the company built for exactly this—those tense domestic moments when a smart mop goes dumb and drowns a rug. Brooke took the day off entirely. She opened windows, tamed the curls, set out a cheese board and an amusing red. Edward came, the super sopper slurped, and his hand slid through her hair as if that were its natural place. Lips brushed, an ear was kissed, and Brooke’s private longings—those age-old tribe feelings that never really grow up—answered back.

Back at the shop, her naughty little appliance blinked a tiny cupid and “Love match,” as if its mishap had been matchmaking all along. Maybe it wasn’t yearning for the sky; maybe Brooke was. Either way, the floors would dry, the birds would flutter, and the future—half digital, half heart—looked suddenly, hilariously bright.
